text: Account Planning Definition Account planning is the structured process of analyzing a customer account to understand its organizational structure, strategic priorities, risks, and growth opportunities. In Customer Success, account planning helps CSMs develop a long-term strategy for guiding the customer relationship, maximizing value realization, and identifying opportunities for expansion. A well-developed account plan provides a clear understanding of the customer’s environment and outlines actions needed to support both customer success and business growth.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Account planning allows Customer Success teams to proactively manage relationships rather than responding only when issues arise. By understanding the customer’s goals, challenges, and internal dynamics, CSMs can provide more strategic guidance and identify opportunities to deepen the partnership. Effective account planning helps organizations: • strengthen customer relationships • identify expansion opportunities • mitigate potential churn risks • align the platform with customer business objectives • improve long-term account stability Strategic account planning transforms Customer Success from a support function into a strategic advisory role.  Customer Psychology Behind Account Planning Customer organizations are influenced by internal priorities, leadership decisions, and evolving business objectives. Understanding these factors allows CSMs to anticipate customer needs and adapt their approach accordingly. Key psychological and organizational dynamics include: Strategic priorities Customers invest in solutions that align with their broader business goals.

Internal advocacy Champions and influential stakeholders play a key role in promoting the product internally. Risk sensitivity Organizations may be cautious when introducing new tools or expanding existing ones. Organizational politics Different departments may have competing priorities that influence decision-making. Understanding these dynamics allows CSMs to navigate complex customer environments more effectively.  Key Components of an Account Plan Customer Business Objectives Understanding the strategic goals and initiatives that matter most to the customer. Stakeholder Mapping Identifying key individuals who influence product adoption, strategy, and budget decisions. Adoption and Usage Insights Evaluating how the platform is currently used across teams and workflows. Risks and Challenges Identifying potential issues such as low adoption, organizational changes, or technical limitations. Growth Opportunities Recognizing areas where the product could provide additional value or expand across the organization. Engagement Strategy Defining how the Customer Success team will maintain relationships with stakeholders and support ongoing success.  Signals of Strong Account Planning • clear understanding of customer goals and priorities • engagement with multiple stakeholders across the organization • proactive identification of risks and opportunities • strategic discussions with customer leadership

• alignment between product capabilities and customer initiatives These signals suggest that the Customer Success team has strong visibility into the account.  Signals of Weak Account Planning • limited visibility into the customer’s strategic goals • reliance on a single contact within the organization • reactive engagement only when issues occur • lack of understanding of internal decision-making processes • missed opportunities for expansion or deeper adoption Weak account planning can result in missed opportunities and unexpected renewal risks.  CSM Actions to Develop Strong Account Plans Research the Customer Organization Understand the company’s industry, strategy, and current challenges. Engage Key Stakeholders Build relationships with individuals across different teams and levels of authority. Align the Platform with Strategic Goals Identify how the product supports the customer’s business priorities. Monitor Account Health and Risks Track adoption, engagement, and satisfaction indicators. Identify Expansion Opportunities Look for ways the platform can support new initiatives or teams within the organization.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate account health and planning effectiveness include: • product adoption levels • stakeholder engagement • expansion pipeline opportunities • customer health scores

• progress toward success plan goals • renewal readiness indicators Monitoring these metrics helps Customer Success teams maintain visibility into account performance and opportunities.  Example Scenario A Customer Success Manager works with a large enterprise customer using a security analytics platform. Through account planning, the CSM identifies that the organization plans to expand its cloud infrastructure over the next year. By aligning the platform’s capabilities with this initiative and engaging both security and infrastructure leaders, the CSM positions the platform as a strategic solution for the company’s future needs.  Strategic Insight Successful Customer Success teams treat customer accounts as evolving partnerships rather than static relationships. By continuously analyzing the customer’s goals, challenges, and organizational dynamics, CSMs can guide the relationship strategically and help customers unlock greater value over time.

Adoption Definition Adoption refers to the extent to which customers actively use a product’s features and capabilities as part of their regular workflows. In Customer Success, adoption indicates whether the product has become integrated into the customer’s operations and is delivering meaningful value. High adoption suggests that users rely on the platform to accomplish important tasks, while low adoption may indicate that the product has not yet been successfully integrated into the customer’s processes.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Product adoption is one of the strongest indicators of long-term customer retention. When customers actively use a platform to support their daily work, they are far more likely to continue investing in the solution. Strong adoption helps organizations: • increase customer retention • accelerate value realization • reduce churn risk • strengthen customer satisfaction • identify opportunities for expansion Conversely, low adoption often signals that customers are struggling to integrate the platform into their workflows, which may lead to disengagement and potential churn.  Customer Psychology Behind Adoption Adoption is influenced not only by product capabilities but also by how users perceive the effort required to change their existing behaviors. Several psychological dynamics influence adoption: Resistance to change Users often prefer familiar tools and processes even when better alternatives exist.

Learning curve anxiety Users may hesitate to adopt new systems if they feel uncertain about their ability to use them effectively. Perceived value Customers are more likely to adopt a product when they clearly understand how it improves their work or solves a meaningful problem. Habit formation Once a product becomes part of daily workflows, adoption tends to stabilize and strengthen over time. Customer Success teams play a key role in helping customers overcome these barriers and integrate the product into their operations.  Signals of Strong Adoption • consistent or increasing product usage • growing number of active users • use of multiple product features • workflows built around the platform • teams relying on the product to complete operational tasks • users requesting training for advanced features These signals indicate that the platform has become embedded in the customer’s environment.  Signals of Weak Adoption • infrequent logins or limited product activity • use of only basic features • reliance on alternative tools or manual processes • users expressing confusion about product functionality • declining engagement with training or enablement sessions Weak adoption may indicate that customers are struggling to understand how the product fits into their workflows.  Common Root Causes of Low Adoption

Unclear Value Proposition Users may not understand how the product improves their work. Insufficient Training Customers may lack the knowledge required to use the platform effectively. Workflow Misalignment The product may not yet be integrated into existing operational processes. Organizational Resistance Internal teams may resist changing established habits. Lack of Executive Support Without leadership endorsement, adoption initiatives may lose priority.  CSM Actions to Improve Adoption Provide Targeted Training Offer onboarding sessions, workshops, and documentation that help users understand how to apply the product. Focus on Key Use Cases Highlight the workflows where the product delivers the greatest value. Identify and Support Champions Empower internal advocates who promote adoption within their teams. Align with Business Outcomes Connect product usage to measurable improvements in efficiency, performance, or risk reduction. Track and Reinforce Progress Regularly review adoption metrics and celebrate milestones with the customer.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate adoption include: • active user counts • login frequency • feature utilization

• workflow completion rates • adoption of advanced capabilities • usage growth over time Tracking these metrics helps Customer Success teams understand how deeply the product is integrated into the customer’s operations.  Example Scenario A company adopts a monitoring platform to improve infrastructure visibility. Initially, only a small group of engineers use the platform for basic reporting. After the Customer Success team conducts targeted training and helps integrate the platform into incident response workflows, adoption increases across the operations team. As more teams rely on the platform for daily tasks, the organization begins to see measurable improvements in operational efficiency.  Strategic Insight Adoption is not simply about using a product—it is about integrating the product into the customer’s workflows and decision-making processes. When a platform becomes essential to daily operations, it is far more likely to deliver sustained value and long-term customer retention.

Business Reviews Definition Business Reviews are structured meetings between a vendor and customer designed to review performance, demonstrate value, discuss progress toward business goals, and align on future priorities. These reviews provide an opportunity for Customer Success teams to communicate outcomes achieved with the platform and ensure continued alignment with the customer’s strategic objectives. Business reviews are commonly conducted quarterly (QBRs) or with executive stakeholders (EBRs).  Why It Matters in Customer Success Business reviews provide a structured forum for discussing the impact of a product beyond day-to-day operational interactions. They allow Customer Success teams to reinforce value, address challenges, and strengthen relationships with both operational users and executive stakeholders. Effective business reviews help organizations: • demonstrate measurable value delivered by the platform • maintain executive alignment • identify opportunities for expansion • address risks before renewal discussions • strengthen trust between the vendor and customer Regular business reviews also ensure that the product remains aligned with the customer’s evolving priorities and business objectives.  Customer Psychology During Business Reviews Stakeholders attending business reviews often want to understand how the product contributes to their organization’s success. Their attention is typically focused on outcomes rather than detailed product functionality. Several psychological dynamics influence business reviews: Outcome focus Executives want to see measurable improvements tied to their business goals.

Time efficiency Stakeholders prefer concise, insight-driven discussions rather than lengthy technical explanations. Strategic relevance Participants expect the conversation to focus on how the platform supports broader organizational priorities. Confidence building Clear communication of results and future opportunities strengthens confidence in the vendor relationship. Understanding these dynamics helps Customer Success teams deliver more impactful business reviews.  Key Components of an Effective Business Review Customer Objectives Revisit the goals and outcomes the customer initially hoped to achieve. Performance Metrics Present data that shows how the product has contributed to measurable improvements. Adoption and Usage Insights Highlight how teams are using the platform and where adoption has grown. Challenges and Risks Identify obstacles or areas that require attention. Future Opportunities Discuss upcoming initiatives, improvements, or expansion possibilities. Strategic Alignment Ensure that the product remains aligned with the customer’s evolving priorities.  Signals of a Successful Business Review • active participation from key stakeholders • discussion focused on business outcomes rather than product features • alignment on next steps or strategic initiatives • executives recognizing the value of the platform

• interest in new capabilities or expansion opportunities These signals indicate that the review is reinforcing the value of the partnership.  Signals of Ineffective Business Reviews • low stakeholder attendance • conversations focused only on product features or technical details • lack of measurable results or insights • minimal discussion about future strategy • limited engagement from executives These signals may indicate that the review is not effectively communicating value.  CSM Actions to Deliver Effective Business Reviews Prepare Data and Insights Gather relevant usage metrics, outcomes, and improvements achieved with the platform. Focus on Business Impact Translate product capabilities into operational or strategic outcomes. Engage Multiple Stakeholders Invite both operational users and executive leaders when appropriate. Encourage Strategic Discussion Use the review to explore future opportunities and challenges. Define Clear Next Steps End the review with agreed-upon actions that support continued success.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators related to business reviews include: • stakeholder attendance and engagement • progress toward success plan milestones • product adoption trends • measurable operational improvements

• expansion opportunities identified during discussions Monitoring these indicators helps ensure that business reviews continue to provide value to the customer relationship.  Example Scenario A Customer Success team conducts a quarterly business review with a company using a cloud monitoring platform. During the meeting, the CSM presents data showing improvements in system uptime and reduced incident response time. The discussion highlights how these improvements support the customer’s operational goals. The review also identifies opportunities to expand the platform to additional infrastructure teams.  Strategic Insight Business reviews are not simply status updates. They are strategic conversations that reinforce value, maintain executive alignment, and shape the future direction of the customer relationship. When conducted effectively, they strengthen trust and position the vendor as a long-term partner in the customer’s success.

Change Management Definition Change management refers to the structured approach used to help individuals, teams, and organizations successfully adopt new processes, technologies, or strategies. In Customer Success, change management focuses on guiding customers through the behavioral and operational adjustments required to fully implement and benefit from a new product or platform. Successful change management ensures that users not only understand the product but also integrate it into their daily workflows.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Most software platforms require customers to modify existing processes, workflows, or habits. Without proper change management, even powerful products may fail to gain adoption. Effective change management helps Customer Success teams: • accelerate product adoption • reduce resistance to new workflows • improve time to value • increase customer satisfaction • reduce churn risk When customers successfully adapt to new systems, they are far more likely to recognize the long-term value of the platform.  Customer Psychology Behind Change Management People naturally resist change, especially when it disrupts familiar workflows or introduces uncertainty. Several psychological factors influence change adoption: Status quo bias Individuals tend to prefer existing processes even if new solutions may offer better outcomes.

Fear of failure Users may hesitate to adopt new tools if they worry about making mistakes or appearing inexperienced. Cognitive overload Learning new systems while maintaining existing responsibilities can feel overwhelming. Loss of control Changes to established workflows may create anxiety if users feel they lack influence over the process. Understanding these dynamics helps CSMs support customers more effectively during implementation and adoption phases.  Signals of Change Resistance • slow adoption of new features or workflows • users reverting to previous tools or processes • limited participation in training or enablement sessions • feedback expressing confusion or frustration • internal teams delaying implementation milestones These signals often indicate that users are struggling to adapt to the new system.  Common Root Causes Lack of Executive Sponsorship Without leadership support, teams may not prioritize the change. Unclear Value Proposition Users may not understand how the product benefits their work. Insufficient Training Users may feel unprepared to use the platform effectively. Process Disruption The product may require changes to established workflows. Poor Communication Stakeholders may not clearly understand why the change is necessary.

CSM Actions to Support Change Management Align with Business Objectives Clearly connect the product to the customer’s goals and strategic initiatives. Develop Internal Champions Identify individuals within the organization who can promote adoption among their peers. Provide Training and Enablement Offer workshops, tutorials, and guidance to help users become comfortable with the platform. Break Implementation into Phases Gradually introduce new workflows rather than overwhelming users with major changes all at once. Communicate Success Stories Highlight early wins and improvements to reinforce the value of the change.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate change management success include: • feature adoption rates • active user growth • workflow completion rates • participation in training sessions • reduction in manual processes • customer feedback regarding usability Tracking these metrics helps Customer Success teams understand how well the organization is adapting to the new system.  Example Scenario A company implements a new security platform designed to automate threat detection and response. However, security analysts continue using legacy tools because they are more familiar with the existing workflow. The CSM works with the customer to conduct training sessions, identify internal champions, and gradually transition key workflows to the new platform. Over time, adoption increases as users become more comfortable with the system.

Strategic Insight Successful product adoption is rarely just a technology challenge—it is primarily a behavioral challenge. Organizations must adjust habits, processes, and expectations before new systems become fully integrated into daily operations. Customer Success teams that understand change management are far better equipped to guide customers through this transition and ensure long-term value realization.

Churn Risk Definition Churn risk refers to the likelihood that a customer will discontinue their use of a product or service at the end of a contract period or reduce their level of engagement or investment. In Customer Success, identifying churn risk early allows teams to intervene proactively and restore customer confidence before the renewal decision occurs. Churn risk often develops gradually as customers experience declining value, unresolved issues, or shifting priorities within their organization.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Retention is a critical driver of growth for SaaS businesses. While acquiring new customers requires significant investment, retaining existing customers allows organizations to sustain predictable recurring revenue and strengthen long-term partnerships. Understanding churn risk helps Customer Success teams: • identify accounts that require immediate attention • address issues before they escalate into renewal challenges • improve customer satisfaction and trust • strengthen relationships with key stakeholders • protect recurring revenue When churn risk is identified early, Customer Success teams can take corrective action and help customers rediscover the value of the platform.  Customer Psychology Behind Churn Risk Customers rarely decide to churn suddenly. Instead, churn typically results from a gradual decline in confidence, engagement, or perceived value. Several psychological dynamics influence churn: Loss of confidence If customers believe the platform no longer solves their problems effectively, their engagement may decrease.

Expectation mismatch Customers may become dissatisfied if the product does not deliver the outcomes they initially expected. Organizational pressure Internal leadership may question the cost or relevance of the platform during budget reviews. Decision fatigue Customers may consider replacing tools when evaluating ways to simplify their technology stack. Understanding these psychological dynamics helps Customer Success teams address concerns before they evolve into churn decisions.  Signals of Churn Risk Behavioral Signals • declining product usage • reduced login frequency • fewer active users • limited feature adoption Engagement Signals • missed meetings or delayed responses • reduced participation in business reviews • declining engagement with Customer Success initiatives Organizational Signals • loss of internal champions • leadership changes within the customer organization • budget scrutiny or cost reduction initiatives These signals often appear long before the renewal conversation begins.  Common Root Causes of Churn Risk Low Product Adoption Customers may not have integrated the platform into their workflows.

Unclear Value Realization Customers may struggle to recognize measurable outcomes from using the product. Weak Stakeholder Coverage If relationships rely on a single contact, the account becomes vulnerable to personnel changes. Unresolved Product Issues Technical problems or support challenges can reduce confidence in the platform. Competitive Alternatives Customers may evaluate other solutions that appear to better address their needs.  CSM Actions to Mitigate Churn Risk Monitor Customer Health Indicators Track adoption, engagement, and satisfaction metrics to identify risk early. Re-establish Value Alignment Reconnect the platform’s capabilities to the customer’s business objectives. Strengthen Stakeholder Relationships Engage additional stakeholders to reduce reliance on a single champion. Resolve Product Issues Quickly Work with support and product teams to address technical challenges. Develop a Recovery Plan Collaborate with the customer to identify actions that restore confidence in the platform.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help identify churn risk include: • product adoption metrics • active user counts • engagement levels with Customer Success teams • support ticket trends • customer health scores • satisfaction or NPS scores

Tracking these metrics helps Customer Success teams proactively identify accounts that may require intervention.  Example Scenario A company adopts a security analytics platform but gradually reduces its usage as the team continues relying on legacy tools. Engagement with the Customer Success team declines, and leadership begins questioning the platform’s value during budget planning. By recognizing these signals early, the CSM schedules a strategic review to realign the platform with the company’s security objectives and restore adoption.  Strategic Insight Churn rarely occurs because of a single problem. Instead, it typically results from a combination of declining engagement, unclear value realization, and organizational changes. Customer Success teams that continuously monitor early signals and address underlying issues can significantly reduce churn risk and strengthen long-term customer relationships.

Customer Advocacy Definition Customer advocacy refers to the process of encouraging and enabling satisfied customers to actively promote, recommend, and support a product or service. In Customer Success, advocacy occurs when customers share positive experiences, participate in case studies, provide testimonials, or act as references for potential buyers. Advocacy transforms customers from product users into trusted supporters who publicly validate the value of the platform.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Customer advocacy strengthens both customer relationships and business growth. When customers are willing to share their success stories, it reinforces trust in the vendor and helps potential customers evaluate the product through real-world experiences. Customer advocacy helps organizations: • build market credibility and trust • support sales through customer references • generate case studies and testimonials • strengthen long-term customer relationships • identify opportunities for expansion and deeper engagement Advocates often become long-term partners who influence both product development and market perception.  Customer Psychology Behind Advocacy Customers typically advocate for products when they feel confident in the value delivered and proud of the outcomes they have achieved. Several psychological factors influence advocacy: Positive outcome recognition Customers who experience measurable improvements are more likely to share their success.

Professional recognition Stakeholders may enjoy being recognized as innovators or leaders within their industry. Trust in the vendor relationship Strong relationships with Customer Success teams increase willingness to participate in advocacy activities. Reciprocity Customers who feel supported and valued by the vendor are more likely to return that support. Understanding these motivations helps Customer Success teams encourage advocacy in a natural and respectful way.  Signals of Strong Advocacy Potential • customers expressing high satisfaction with the product • strong adoption and value realization • active engagement in business reviews and strategic discussions • willingness to provide feedback or participate in product discussions • customers recommending the platform internally or externally These signals often indicate that customers may be open to participating in advocacy initiatives.  Signals of Low Advocacy Potential • unresolved product issues or dissatisfaction • limited adoption or unclear value realization • weak engagement with Customer Success teams • lack of executive support for the platform Customers must first experience clear value before they feel comfortable advocating for a product.  Common Forms of Customer Advocacy Customer References Customers speak with prospective buyers about their experience with the product.

Case Studies Documented stories describing how the customer achieved success using the platform. Testimonials Short statements highlighting the value or benefits of the product. Conference or Webinar Participation Customers share their experiences at industry events or online sessions. Product Feedback and Advisory Boards Customers provide strategic feedback that helps shape product development.  CSM Actions to Encourage Customer Advocacy Deliver Consistent Value Advocacy begins with strong adoption and measurable outcomes. Identify Potential Advocates Recognize customers who demonstrate strong satisfaction and engagement. Provide Opportunities for Recognition Offer platforms where customers can share their success stories. Support Customer Champions Help internal advocates showcase the impact of the product within their organization. Coordinate with Marketing and Sales Teams Collaborate with internal teams to identify appropriate advocacy opportunities.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators related to customer advocacy include: • number of customer references provided • case studies or testimonials created • participation in webinars or industry events • customer satisfaction or NPS scores • referrals or new opportunities influenced by existing customers Tracking these indicators helps organizations understand the impact of advocacy programs.

Example Scenario A company successfully implements a cybersecurity platform that significantly improves its threat detection capabilities. After achieving measurable improvements in operational efficiency, the organization agrees to participate in a case study and provides references for potential customers evaluating the platform. Their success story helps other organizations understand the platform’s real-world impact.  Strategic Insight Customer advocacy represents the highest level of customer success. When customers willingly promote a product and share their success stories, it reflects deep trust in the vendor relationship and strong confidence in the value delivered. Advocacy transforms satisfied customers into influential partners who contribute to both product growth and market credibility.

Customer Health Definition Customer health refers to the overall status of a customer relationship based on a combination of behavioral, operational, and relationship indicators. In Customer Success, health scoring systems are used to evaluate whether a customer is likely to continue realizing value from a product and renew their contract. Customer health typically reflects multiple factors, including product adoption, engagement, satisfaction, and alignment with business goals.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Customer health provides Customer Success teams with a structured way to monitor accounts and identify potential risks or opportunities. By tracking health indicators, teams can proactively address challenges before they escalate into churn or dissatisfaction. Effective customer health monitoring helps organizations: • identify accounts at risk of churn • prioritize Customer Success resources • detect expansion opportunities • track value realization • maintain long-term customer relationships Health scoring also allows organizations to move from reactive support to proactive customer management.  Customer Psychology Behind Customer Health Customer health reflects not only product usage but also how customers feel about the value of the relationship. Several psychological factors influence customer health: Confidence in the solution Customers who believe the platform reliably supports their goals are more likely to remain committed to the relationship.

Trust in the vendor Positive interactions with Customer Success teams reinforce trust and strengthen long-term partnerships. Perceived return on investment Customers must feel that the benefits of the platform justify the cost and effort required to maintain it. Internal alignment When internal stakeholders agree on the product’s value, the relationship tends to remain stable. When these psychological elements weaken, overall customer health often declines.  Signals of Strong Customer Health • consistent or increasing product adoption • active engagement in success meetings • executive support for the platform • positive feedback or satisfaction scores • customers achieving measurable business outcomes • interest in new capabilities or expansion opportunities Healthy customers typically demonstrate strong relationships, consistent product usage, and clear recognition of value.  Signals of Poor Customer Health • declining product usage or adoption • reduced communication or engagement • unresolved support issues • stakeholders questioning the product’s value • leadership changes or shifting priorities • delayed responses to success initiatives These signals may indicate that the customer relationship requires immediate attention.  Common Root Causes of Health Decline

Low Product Adoption Customers may struggle to integrate the platform into their workflows. Weak Value Realization Customers may not clearly understand how the product contributes to their business outcomes. Internal Stakeholder Changes New leadership may reassess existing tools and budgets. Unresolved Technical Issues Persistent product issues can reduce confidence in the platform. Communication Gaps Limited communication between the vendor and the customer may prevent problems from being addressed early.  CSM Actions to Improve Customer Health Monitor Health Indicators Regularly Track adoption metrics, engagement signals, and feedback to identify trends. Address Risks Early Investigate declining health signals before they become serious problems. Reinforce Value Regularly communicate measurable outcomes and improvements achieved with the platform. Strengthen Relationships Maintain communication with both operational users and executive stakeholders. Create Success Plans Develop clear strategies that help customers achieve their desired business outcomes.  Metrics to Monitor Customer health often incorporates multiple indicators, such as: • product adoption rates • number of active users • feature utilization • customer engagement levels

• support ticket volume • customer satisfaction or NPS scores • renewal and expansion indicators Many organizations combine these metrics into a health scoring model to evaluate account stability.  Example Scenario A customer initially adopts a software platform with strong enthusiasm and engagement. Over time, product usage declines and key stakeholders stop attending success meetings. Support tickets remain unresolved and leadership begins questioning the platform’s value. These combined signals indicate declining customer health and require immediate attention from the Customer Success team.  Strategic Insight Customer health is rarely determined by a single metric. Instead, it reflects the combined strength of adoption, engagement, value realization, and stakeholder alignment. Successful Customer Success teams continuously monitor these signals and intervene early to maintain strong customer relationships.

Customer Journey Definition The customer journey refers to the complete lifecycle of a customer’s experience with a product or service, from the initial purchase and onboarding process through adoption, value realization, renewal, and potential expansion. In Customer Success, understanding the customer journey helps teams guide customers through each stage of their relationship with the vendor. Each stage of the journey presents different opportunities, risks, and engagement strategies that influence long-term customer success.  Why It Matters in Customer Success A structured customer journey enables Customer Success teams to proactively manage the customer relationship rather than reacting to issues as they arise. Understanding the customer journey helps organizations: • guide customers through successful onboarding and adoption • anticipate challenges at different stages of the lifecycle • identify opportunities to deliver value and strengthen relationships • reduce churn risk during critical moments • create opportunities for renewal and expansion When Customer Success teams manage the journey effectively, customers are more likely to achieve meaningful outcomes and remain long-term partners.  Customer Psychology Throughout the Journey Customer expectations and priorities often change throughout the lifecycle of the relationship. Recognizing these shifts allows Customer Success teams to tailor their approach at each stage. Early Stage – Excitement and Expectations Customers are optimistic about the product and expect quick improvements or results. Adoption Stage – Learning and Adjustment Users begin integrating the product into their workflows and may encounter challenges or resistance.

Value Realization Stage – Confidence Building Customers begin recognizing measurable outcomes and feel more confident in their investment. Renewal Stage – Evaluation and Justification Customers evaluate whether the product continues to deliver value relative to its cost. Understanding these psychological transitions helps Customer Success teams provide the right support at the right time.  Key Stages of the Customer Journey Onboarding The initial implementation and setup of the product. This stage focuses on helping customers understand how the platform works and preparing them to begin using it effectively. Adoption Customers integrate the product into their daily workflows and begin using key features regularly. Value Realization Customers start seeing measurable improvements or outcomes that demonstrate the product’s impact. Renewal Customers evaluate whether to continue their investment in the platform based on the value they have experienced. Expansion Customers deepen their investment by increasing usage, adding new features, or expanding adoption across teams.  Signals of a Healthy Customer Journey • smooth onboarding and implementation • increasing product adoption over time • strong engagement with Customer Success teams

• measurable business outcomes achieved with the platform • positive feedback and executive support • interest in additional capabilities or expansion opportunities These signals suggest that the customer relationship is progressing successfully through the lifecycle.  Signals of Journey Breakdown • stalled onboarding or implementation delays • limited adoption of key product features • declining engagement with Customer Success teams • lack of measurable value or outcomes • customers questioning the product’s relevance during renewal discussions Breakdowns at earlier stages often create risk later in the lifecycle.  CSM Actions to Guide the Customer Journey Establish Clear Onboarding Milestones Define implementation goals and timelines to help customers reach initial success quickly. Support Product Adoption Provide training and enablement resources to help users integrate the product into their workflows. Track and Communicate Value Demonstrate measurable outcomes that reinforce the product’s impact. Maintain Regular Engagement Schedule success meetings, strategy sessions, and QBRs to maintain alignment. Prepare for Renewal Early Ensure customers recognize the product’s value well before renewal discussions begin.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate progress through the customer journey include:

• onboarding completion rates • time to value • product adoption metrics • engagement levels • customer health scores • renewal and expansion indicators Tracking these metrics helps Customer Success teams identify where customers are in the lifecycle and where additional support may be required.  Example Scenario A company adopts a new analytics platform to improve operational visibility. During onboarding, the Customer Success team helps implement core dashboards and train key users. Over time, adoption spreads across departments as teams recognize the platform’s ability to streamline reporting and decision-making. As measurable improvements become clear, executives support renewal and expand the platform to additional business units.  Strategic Insight Customer success does not happen at a single moment—it develops across the entire lifecycle of the relationship. Organizations that intentionally guide customers through each stage of the journey are far more likely to achieve strong retention, deeper adoption, and long-term partnerships.  Save as: customer-journey.pdf  What Your CSM Brain Now Includes You now have 12 core Customer Success concepts: • Adoption • Churn Risk • Executive Alignment • Expansion • Engagement
• Escalations • Change Management • Customer Health • Stakeholder Mapping • Time to Value • Value Realization • Customer Journey This is now a solid strategic framework for Customer Success thinking.

Customer Maturity Model Definition A Customer Maturity Model is a framework used to assess how advanced a customer is in their adoption, usage, and operational integration of a product or platform. It helps Customer Success teams evaluate the customer’s current capabilities and guide them toward higher levels of maturity and value realization. The maturity model typically outlines stages that represent increasing levels of product adoption, operational integration, and strategic impact.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Not all customers use a product in the same way or at the same level of sophistication. Some customers may only use basic features, while others integrate the platform deeply into their operations. Understanding customer maturity allows Customer Success teams to: • tailor guidance and recommendations • set realistic expectations for adoption • prioritize enablement and training efforts • identify opportunities for expansion • help customers progress toward more advanced use cases By understanding where a customer currently stands, CSMs can develop strategies that help the organization unlock greater value over time.  Customer Psychology Behind Maturity Progression Customers typically move through stages of learning and confidence as they adopt a new platform. Several psychological dynamics influence maturity progression: Learning curve Users gradually build familiarity and confidence with the platform.

Trust development As customers experience successful outcomes, their confidence in the product grows. Operational dependency When the platform becomes embedded in workflows, customers rely on it more heavily. Strategic recognition Leadership begins to view the product as a strategic asset rather than just a tool. Recognizing these psychological shifts helps Customer Success teams guide customers through each stage of maturity.  Typical Stages of Customer Maturity Initial Adoption Customers are learning how the product works and beginning to implement basic workflows. Characteristics: • limited feature usage • small group of users • early experimentation with the platform  Operational Use The platform becomes part of daily workflows and is used consistently by key teams. Characteristics: • increasing user adoption • regular usage of core features • measurable operational improvements  Optimization Customers begin exploring advanced capabilities and refining workflows to maximize efficiency.

Characteristics: • deeper feature usage • process improvements enabled by the platform • growing internal advocacy  Strategic Integration The platform becomes deeply integrated into the organization’s operations and decision-making processes. Characteristics: • cross-departmental adoption • strong executive alignment • platform supports strategic initiatives  Signals of High Customer Maturity • widespread adoption across teams • consistent use of advanced product features • strong executive support • measurable business outcomes tied to the platform • interest in expansion or new capabilities Customers at this stage often become advocates and long-term partners.  Signals of Low Customer Maturity • limited feature adoption • small number of users • unclear understanding of product capabilities • reliance on basic functionality only • minimal engagement with Customer Success initiatives These signals suggest that the customer may require additional guidance and enablement.

CSM Actions to Support Maturity Growth Assess the Customer’s Current Stage Understand how the customer currently uses the platform and their level of expertise. Provide Targeted Enablement Offer training and guidance appropriate for the customer’s maturity level. Introduce Advanced Use Cases Help customers discover new capabilities that align with their evolving needs. Promote Strategic Conversations Encourage discussions about how the platform can support broader business goals. Track Progress Over Time Monitor improvements in adoption, engagement, and operational outcomes.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate customer maturity include: • feature adoption rates • number of active users • depth of product usage • workflow integration • cross-team adoption • engagement with advanced capabilities These metrics help Customer Success teams understand how the customer’s use of the platform evolves over time.  Example Scenario A company initially adopts a cybersecurity platform to monitor network activity. Over time, the organization expands its use of the platform to automate threat detection and integrate security data across multiple departments. As adoption grows and leadership begins using insights from the platform for strategic planning, the customer progresses from basic operational use to strategic integration.

Strategic Insight Customers rarely achieve full value from a platform immediately after implementation. Value grows as customers progress through stages of maturity. Customer Success teams that actively guide customers through this progression help unlock deeper operational impact and long-term partnerships.

Customer Segmentation Definition Customer segmentation is the process of grouping customers into categories based on shared characteristics such as company size, revenue potential, product usage, industry, or strategic importance. In Customer Success, segmentation helps organizations determine the level of support, engagement model, and resources allocated to different customer groups. Segmentation enables Customer Success teams to deliver tailored experiences that align with the needs and value of each customer segment.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Not all customers require the same level of engagement or support. Some accounts may need dedicated strategic guidance, while others may benefit from automated or scalable support models. Customer segmentation helps organizations: • prioritize Customer Success resources effectively • provide tailored engagement strategies for different customer groups • improve operational efficiency • scale Customer Success programs as the customer base grows • focus strategic attention on high-value accounts By aligning engagement strategies with customer needs and potential value, Customer Success teams can deliver more impactful support.  Customer Psychology Behind Segmentation Customers often expect a level of service that reflects the scale of their investment and relationship with the vendor. Several psychological dynamics influence how customers perceive segmentation: Perceived importance Customers who invest heavily in a platform expect more personalized attention.

Service expectations Different customer segments may have different expectations regarding responsiveness and support. Relationship value Customers who feel recognized and supported are more likely to maintain long-term relationships with the vendor. Efficiency preferences Some customers prefer self-service resources rather than frequent direct engagement. Understanding these expectations helps Customer Success teams design engagement models that meet the needs of each segment.  Common Customer Segmentation Models Enterprise Segment Large organizations with significant contracts and strategic partnerships. Characteristics: • high revenue value • complex organizational structures • dedicated Customer Success Manager • frequent strategic engagement  Mid-Market Segment Medium-sized organizations with growing product usage and potential for expansion. Characteristics: • moderate contract value • increasing product adoption • periodic strategic engagement • opportunities for expansion  SMB Segment

Smaller organizations that often require scalable engagement models. Characteristics: • lower contract value • smaller user groups • greater reliance on self-service resources • automated onboarding and support programs  Signals of Effective Segmentation • clear engagement models for each customer segment • appropriate allocation of Customer Success resources • tailored communication and support strategies • improved efficiency in managing large customer bases • strong retention and satisfaction across segments These signals indicate that segmentation strategies are supporting both operational efficiency and customer success.  Signals of Ineffective Segmentation • Customer Success teams overwhelmed with too many accounts • inconsistent engagement across customers • high-value accounts receiving insufficient attention • smaller customers receiving more support than necessary • unclear criteria for assigning accounts to segments Ineffective segmentation can reduce both customer satisfaction and operational efficiency.  CSM Actions to Support Effective Segmentation Understand Customer Profiles Analyze account size, industry, and strategic importance. Align Engagement Strategies with Segments Adjust communication frequency, resources, and support models based on the segment.

Monitor Customer Growth Customers may move between segments as their usage or investment increases. Provide Scalable Resources Offer self-service materials such as documentation, webinars, and community forums for smaller segments. Maintain Strategic Focus Ensure that high-value accounts receive the attention needed to support long-term partnerships.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate segmentation effectiveness include: • retention rates across segments • customer satisfaction levels • expansion revenue by segment • engagement levels within each segment • CSM account load and efficiency Tracking these metrics helps organizations refine their segmentation strategies over time.  Example Scenario A software company supports a diverse customer base ranging from small startups to large enterprise organizations. Enterprise customers receive dedicated Customer Success Managers and quarterly strategic reviews, while smaller customers access self-service training and automated support resources. By segmenting customers based on contract value and complexity, the company ensures that resources are allocated effectively.  Strategic Insight Customer segmentation allows organizations to scale Customer Success without sacrificing quality. By tailoring engagement models to different customer needs and value levels, companies can deliver the right level of support while maintaining operational efficiency and strong customer relationships.

Engagement Definition Engagement refers to the level of interaction and involvement a customer has with a product, the Customer Success team, and related success initiatives. In Customer Success, engagement measures how actively customers participate in meetings, training, product usage, feedback discussions, and strategic planning. High engagement indicates that customers are invested in the partnership and are actively working toward achieving value from the platform.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Customer engagement is a strong indicator of relationship health and long-term retention. Customers who regularly communicate with the vendor, attend strategic reviews, and participate in enablement initiatives are more likely to achieve successful outcomes. Strong engagement helps organizations: • strengthen customer relationships • improve product adoption • identify risks earlier • increase value realization • create opportunities for expansion Low engagement can make it difficult for Customer Success teams to understand customer needs or address challenges before they escalate.  Customer Psychology Behind Engagement Engagement often reflects how customers perceive the value and relevance of the product within their organization. Several psychological dynamics influence engagement: Perceived value Customers are more likely to engage when they believe the platform is helping them achieve meaningful outcomes.

Trust in the vendor relationship Customers who trust the Customer Success team are more open to discussions, collaboration, and feedback. Time prioritization Busy stakeholders will only engage when interactions are perceived as useful and relevant. Motivation for improvement Customers who want to optimize their workflows or outcomes are more likely to participate in training and strategic discussions. Understanding these motivations helps CSMs design interactions that customers find worthwhile.  Signals of Strong Engagement • customers respond promptly to communications • stakeholders attend business reviews or success meetings • active participation in training or enablement sessions • willingness to discuss challenges and strategic initiatives • customers providing feedback or product suggestions These signals suggest that the customer is invested in the success of the partnership.  Signals of Weak Engagement • delayed or inconsistent responses to communication • missed meetings or cancellations • limited participation in business reviews • reluctance to discuss product usage or strategy • declining interaction with Customer Success initiatives Weak engagement may indicate declining interest in the platform or competing priorities within the organization.  Common Root Causes of Low Engagement Limited Perceived Value Customers may not clearly see how the product supports their objectives.

Stakeholder Turnover Loss of champions or key contacts can reduce communication and collaboration. Competing Organizational Priorities Customers may shift attention to other initiatives or tools. Lack of Structured Communication Without regular touchpoints, engagement may gradually decline. Product Adoption Challenges Users may disengage if they struggle to use the platform effectively.  CSM Actions to Improve Engagement Establish Regular Touchpoints Schedule recurring meetings to maintain communication and alignment. Deliver Relevant Insights Provide meaningful data or recommendations that help customers improve outcomes. Encourage Collaboration Create opportunities for customers to share feedback, questions, and ideas. Engage Multiple Stakeholders Build relationships with several contacts to strengthen account coverage. Demonstrate Value Consistently Show how the platform contributes to operational or strategic improvements.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate engagement include: • response rates to communications • attendance at business reviews or meetings • participation in training sessions • number of customer interactions • feedback submissions or survey participation Tracking these metrics helps Customer Success teams identify changes in engagement patterns.

Example Scenario A company uses a cloud monitoring platform to support its operations team. Initially, the Customer Success team meets regularly with the organization to review performance metrics and provide guidance. Over time, engagement begins to decline as meetings are missed and responses slow. The CSM identifies the issue and reconnects with new stakeholders, helping re-establish communication and restore engagement.  Strategic Insight Engagement is a critical foundation for successful Customer Success relationships. When customers actively communicate, collaborate, and participate in success initiatives, it becomes easier to guide them toward adoption, value realization, and long-term partnership. Consistent engagement allows Customer Success teams to understand evolving customer needs and provide proactive support throughout the customer lifecycle.

Escalations Definition An escalation occurs when a customer issue becomes urgent, high-impact, or unresolved through normal support channels and requires additional attention from senior staff, cross-functional teams, or leadership. Escalations often involve significant customer frustration, operational disruption, or business risk. In Customer Success, escalations require careful coordination between technical teams, support, product, and leadership to resolve the issue while maintaining customer trust.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Escalations can significantly impact customer relationships and renewal outcomes. If handled poorly, they may lead to loss of trust, reputational damage, or customer churn. However, when managed effectively, escalations can actually strengthen the relationship by demonstrating responsiveness, accountability, and commitment to the customer’s success. Escalation management allows Customer Success teams to: • address critical issues quickly • maintain transparency with stakeholders • coordinate internal resources effectively • restore customer confidence Successful resolution of escalations often reinforces the customer’s perception of the vendor as a reliable partner.  Customer Psychology During Escalations When issues escalate, customers are often under pressure from internal stakeholders. Their frustration may reflect broader organizational stress rather than dissatisfaction with the CSM personally. Key psychological dynamics during escalations include: Urgency and stress Customers may feel responsible for resolving the issue quickly, which can increase emotional intensity.

Loss of trust If the problem has persisted without resolution, customers may begin questioning the reliability of the platform or vendor. Internal accountability Customer stakeholders may be facing pressure from leadership or their own teams to resolve the issue. Expectation of ownership Customers expect vendors to take responsibility and communicate clearly about resolution progress. Understanding these dynamics helps CSMs respond with empathy and professionalism while focusing on solutions.  Signals That an Escalation Is Occurring • repeated complaints or urgent requests from the customer • involvement of senior stakeholders or executives • requests for immediate meetings or urgent updates • increased support ticket volume or severity • customers expressing frustration or dissatisfaction • threats of contract cancellation or renewal risk Early recognition of escalation signals allows CSMs to intervene before the situation worsens.  Common Root Causes Technical Issues System outages, bugs, or integration failures. Product Limitations Customer expectations exceed the platform’s capabilities. Communication Breakdowns Delayed responses or unclear updates create frustration. Implementation Challenges Problems during onboarding or deployment.

Expectation Misalignment Customers believe the product should deliver outcomes that were not clearly defined during the sales process.  CSM Actions During an Escalation Acknowledge the Issue Quickly Respond promptly and recognize the customer’s concern. Take Ownership Ensure the customer feels that the issue is being actively managed. Coordinate Internal Teams Engage support, engineering, product, and leadership as necessary. Provide Clear Communication Share regular updates and set realistic expectations for resolution. Document the Issue Track progress, root causes, and actions taken to ensure transparency. Focus on Resolution and Recovery Once resolved, discuss preventative measures and reinforce trust with the customer.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators related to escalations may include: • number of support tickets • severity level of incidents • resolution time • customer satisfaction following incident resolution • frequency of repeated issues • escalation rate within accounts Monitoring these metrics helps organizations identify patterns that may require product improvements or process changes.  Example Scenario

A customer experiences a platform outage that prevents their operations team from completing critical workflows. After multiple support tickets and delayed responses, the customer escalates the issue to executive leadership. The CSM organizes an urgent meeting with the customer, support, and engineering teams to provide updates and coordinate resolution. Clear communication and rapid response help restore trust once the issue is resolved.  Strategic Insight Escalations are not just technical incidents—they are relationship events. How a company communicates, takes responsibility, and resolves problems during difficult moments often determines whether a customer relationship deteriorates or becomes stronger. Effective escalation management focuses on both technical resolution and relationship recovery.

Executive Alignment Definition Executive alignment refers to the degree to which senior leaders within a customer organization understand, support, and remain committed to the strategic value of a product or service. In Customer Success, executive alignment ensures that the platform is connected to the organization’s broader business objectives and continues to receive leadership support. When executive stakeholders recognize the value and strategic importance of a platform, they are more likely to support renewals, expansion initiatives, and organizational adoption.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Executives typically control budgets, strategic priorities, and long-term investment decisions. Without executive alignment, even well-adopted products can face scrutiny during budget reviews or leadership changes. Strong executive alignment helps organizations: • secure renewal approval • maintain leadership support for adoption initiatives • reinforce the product’s strategic importance • enable expansion opportunities across departments • reduce churn risk during organizational changes When leadership understands the value of the platform, the product becomes part of the organization’s strategic infrastructure rather than a discretionary tool.  Customer Psychology Behind Executive Alignment Executives approach technology investments differently than operational users. While users often focus on features and workflows, executives prioritize outcomes and strategic impact. Several psychological dynamics influence executive alignment: Outcome orientation Executives evaluate tools based on measurable business outcomes such as cost reduction, operational efficiency, or risk mitigation.

Time constraints Senior leaders typically prefer concise, high-level insights rather than detailed technical explanations. Risk management Executives are responsible for organizational decisions and often prioritize solutions that reduce uncertainty or operational risk. Strategic relevance Products that clearly support company initiatives or long-term strategy are more likely to maintain leadership support. Understanding these dynamics helps CSMs communicate value in ways that resonate with executive stakeholders.  Signals of Strong Executive Alignment • executives attend QBRs or executive business reviews • leadership references the platform during strategic discussions • internal teams receive executive support for adoption initiatives • executives advocate for continued investment in the platform • the product is linked to measurable business outcomes These signals suggest that the platform is viewed as strategically important.  Signals of Weak Executive Alignment • executives rarely participate in strategic meetings • operational users struggle to explain the platform’s value to leadership • the product is viewed as a tactical tool rather than a strategic asset • leadership questions the necessity or cost of the platform • renewal discussions occur without prior executive engagement Weak executive alignment often creates uncertainty during renewal cycles.  Common Root Causes Lack of Value Communication The impact of the platform is not clearly communicated to leadership.

Limited Stakeholder Coverage The vendor relationship may rely primarily on operational users rather than decision makers. Misalignment with Business Goals The product’s capabilities may not be clearly connected to the organization’s strategic priorities. Leadership Changes New executives may reassess existing tools and budgets. Insufficient Executive Engagement Executives may not have opportunities to discuss strategic value or outcomes.  CSM Actions to Improve Executive Alignment Translate Product Capabilities into Business Outcomes Explain how the platform contributes to measurable improvements. Engage Executive Stakeholders Early Build relationships with decision makers before renewal conversations begin. Deliver Executive-Level Communication Provide concise summaries focused on outcomes rather than technical details. Facilitate Strategic Reviews Use executive business reviews to align the platform with evolving business priorities. Support Internal Champions Help champions communicate the product’s value to leadership.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate executive alignment include: • executive participation in strategic reviews • executive engagement during QBRs or EBRs • expansion approvals or investment increases • internal sponsorship from leadership • alignment between platform usage and business objectives Monitoring these indicators helps Customer Success teams understand whether leadership recognizes the platform’s value.

Example Scenario A company adopts a cloud security platform used primarily by the security operations team. While operational users rely on the platform daily, executive leadership has limited visibility into its impact. During budget planning, leadership questions the cost of the platform because its strategic value has not been clearly communicated. The Customer Success team organizes an executive business review to demonstrate improvements in threat detection and operational efficiency, helping leadership recognize the platform’s value.  Strategic Insight Strong executive alignment ensures that a product is seen as a strategic investment rather than an optional tool. When leadership understands how a platform contributes to business outcomes, renewal and expansion discussions become significantly easier because the product is already recognized as valuable to the organization.

Expansion Definition Expansion refers to the growth of an existing customer account through increased product usage, additional product purchases, new feature adoption, or deployment across additional teams or departments. In Customer Success, expansion occurs when customers recognize the value of a platform and choose to deepen their investment in the solution. Expansion is often driven by successful adoption, measurable outcomes, and strong relationships between the vendor and customer.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Expansion revenue is one of the primary drivers of growth for SaaS companies. It allows organizations to increase revenue from existing customers without the cost and effort required to acquire new ones. Effective expansion strategies help organizations: • increase annual recurring revenue (ARR) • strengthen long-term customer partnerships • reinforce the value of the platform • deepen adoption across the organization • improve customer lifetime value Customers who expand their investment typically have strong confidence in the platform and its impact on their operations.  Customer Psychology Behind Expansion Expansion decisions are influenced by how customers perceive the value and reliability of a product. Several psychological dynamics influence expansion opportunities: Confidence in value delivered Customers are more willing to expand when they have already experienced clear benefits.

Risk reduction Customers often prefer expanding existing trusted tools rather than introducing entirely new solutions. Operational dependency As teams rely more heavily on the platform, expanding its use becomes a natural progression. Internal advocacy Champions who have seen success with the platform may recommend it to other teams within the organization. Understanding these dynamics helps Customer Success teams identify expansion opportunities that align with customer needs.  Signals of Expansion Opportunity • increasing product adoption across teams • interest in advanced features or capabilities • internal champions promoting the platform • new business initiatives that align with product capabilities • executives discussing broader strategic use of the platform These signals indicate that customers may be ready to expand their investment.  Signals of Limited Expansion Potential • low or stagnant product adoption • unclear value realization • limited engagement with Customer Success teams • unresolved product issues or dissatisfaction • lack of executive support for the platform Expansion rarely occurs when customers are still struggling to achieve success with their current implementation.  Common Root Causes of Missed Expansion Opportunities Limited Visibility into Customer Initiatives CSMs may not be aware of new projects or strategic initiatives within the customer organization.

Weak Stakeholder Coverage Expansion opportunities may exist in departments where the CSM has limited relationships. Incomplete Value Communication Customers may not realize the full range of capabilities the platform offers. Focus on Support Rather Than Strategy Customer Success teams may focus on operational issues instead of identifying growth opportunities.  CSM Actions to Drive Expansion Strengthen Adoption First Expansion is most successful when the existing implementation is delivering strong value. Identify Strategic Initiatives Learn about the customer’s upcoming projects and organizational priorities. Expand Stakeholder Relationships Build connections with teams that could benefit from the platform. Highlight Advanced Capabilities Introduce features or solutions that align with evolving customer needs. Partner with Sales Teams Collaborate with account executives to structure expansion opportunities that align with customer goals.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help identify expansion potential include: • growth in active users • feature adoption trends • cross-team platform usage • engagement in business reviews • customer health scores • expansion pipeline opportunities Tracking these metrics helps Customer Success teams identify when customers may be ready to increase their investment.

Example Scenario A company initially adopts a cloud security platform to monitor network activity. After successfully integrating the platform into its security operations workflows, the organization begins exploring additional capabilities such as automated threat detection and cloud security monitoring. As new teams recognize the value of the platform, the company expands its deployment across multiple departments.  Strategic Insight Expansion is rarely the result of direct sales efforts alone. It typically emerges when customers consistently experience value, trust the vendor relationship, and recognize opportunities to apply the platform more broadly. Customer Success teams that focus on delivering outcomes and building strong relationships naturally create the conditions for expansion.

Renewal Strategy Definition Renewal strategy refers to the structured approach used by Customer Success teams to ensure that customers continue their investment in a product or service at the end of a contract period. In Customer Success, renewal strategy focuses on reinforcing value, maintaining stakeholder alignment, and addressing risks well before the renewal date. A successful renewal strategy ensures that customers view renewal as a natural continuation of the partnership rather than a decision point.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Renewals are the foundation of recurring revenue for SaaS companies. While acquiring new customers drives growth, retaining existing customers is often more efficient and cost-effective. Effective renewal strategies help organizations: • maintain predictable recurring revenue • strengthen long-term customer relationships • reduce churn risk • create opportunities for expansion • reinforce the value delivered throughout the customer lifecycle When renewal conversations occur after customers have consistently experienced value, they are typically smoother and more predictable.  Customer Psychology Behind Renewals Customers evaluate renewals based on their perception of value, trust in the vendor relationship, and confidence in the product’s future impact. Several psychological dynamics influence renewal decisions: Value justification Stakeholders must be able to explain and defend the investment internally.

Risk evaluation Customers consider the potential risk of discontinuing the product compared to the benefits of continuing. Familiarity and operational dependency Products that become embedded in workflows are more difficult to replace. Executive confidence Leadership must believe that the platform contributes to strategic goals and operational improvements. Customer Success teams play a key role in ensuring that customers recognize the value delivered throughout the contract period.  Signals of Strong Renewal Position • consistent product adoption and usage • measurable business outcomes achieved • strong engagement with Customer Success teams • executive alignment with the platform’s value • interest in expansion or additional capabilities • internal champions advocating for the product These signals suggest that the customer sees continued value in the partnership.  Signals of Renewal Risk • declining product usage or engagement • unresolved support issues • stakeholders questioning ROI or value • loss of internal champions or executive sponsors • delayed responses to renewal discussions • increasing competitive interest or evaluation of alternatives Early identification of these signals allows Customer Success teams to intervene and address concerns.  Common Renewal Challenges

Weak Value Communication Customers may not clearly recognize the outcomes achieved during the contract period. Limited Stakeholder Coverage Renewal discussions may involve stakeholders who were not previously engaged. Leadership Changes New decision makers may reassess existing tools and budgets. Budget Constraints Organizations may face financial pressures that require justification for continued investment. Competitive Pressure Customers may consider alternative solutions.  CSM Actions to Support Successful Renewals Start Renewal Conversations Early Begin discussing renewal value and outcomes well before the contract expiration date. Demonstrate Measurable Value Highlight improvements, efficiencies, or outcomes achieved with the platform. Maintain Executive Alignment Ensure leadership understands the strategic importance of the product. Address Risks Proactively Identify and resolve issues before they become obstacles to renewal. Prepare Internal Champions Support stakeholders who advocate for the product within their organization.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help assess renewal readiness include: • product adoption trends • customer health score • engagement with success meetings • satisfaction or NPS scores • resolution of support issues

• renewal pipeline indicators Tracking these metrics helps Customer Success teams understand the likelihood of renewal and prioritize at-risk accounts.  Example Scenario A company uses a cloud monitoring platform to improve infrastructure visibility. Throughout the contract period, the Customer Success team tracks improvements in system uptime and operational efficiency. Before the renewal date, the CSM presents a summary of measurable outcomes achieved with the platform and discusses future opportunities for expansion. Because the customer clearly recognizes the value delivered, the renewal proceeds smoothly.  Strategic Insight Renewals should not be treated as isolated events that occur at the end of a contract. Instead, they are the result of consistent value delivery, strong relationships, and strategic alignment developed throughout the entire customer lifecycle. When customers experience ongoing value and trust the vendor relationship, renewal becomes the natural outcome of the partnership.

Time to Value Definition Time to Value (TTV) refers to the amount of time it takes for a customer to realize meaningful benefits from a product or service after implementation. In Customer Success, reducing time to value is critical because early positive outcomes reinforce the customer’s confidence in the product and vendor relationship. Time to Value is closely connected to onboarding, product adoption, and the customer’s ability to integrate the platform into their workflows.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Customers often purchase software with the expectation that it will quickly improve their operations, solve problems, or deliver measurable outcomes. If customers do not experience value within a reasonable timeframe, they may begin questioning the effectiveness of the solution. Reducing time to value helps Customer Success teams: • accelerate product adoption • increase customer satisfaction • strengthen early customer confidence • reduce churn risk • create opportunities for expansion Early success builds momentum and encourages customers to deepen their investment in the platform.  Customer Psychology Behind Time to Value Customers typically evaluate new technologies based on how quickly they see tangible results. When value is delayed, uncertainty and skepticism may increase. Several psychological factors influence perceptions of value: Expectation fulfillment Customers expect the product to deliver the benefits promised during the sales process.

Confidence building Early success increases trust in both the product and the vendor. Motivation and momentum Quick wins create positive momentum that encourages users to continue adopting the platform. Perceived investment return Stakeholders want to justify the time, effort, and cost invested in implementing the product. When customers see early results, they become more engaged and motivated to expand their usage.  Signals of Strong Time to Value • customers achieving early milestones during onboarding • successful completion of initial workflows • measurable improvements in operational efficiency • positive feedback shortly after implementation • increasing product adoption during early stages These signals suggest that customers are beginning to experience meaningful benefits from the platform.  Signals of Delayed Time to Value • long or stalled implementation timelines • incomplete onboarding processes • users unsure how to apply the product to their workflows • declining engagement shortly after onboarding • customers questioning the product’s value early in the relationship Delayed value realization may increase the likelihood of churn.  Common Root Causes Complex Implementation Integration challenges or technical dependencies may slow deployment.

Unclear Success Criteria Customers may not clearly understand what success looks like with the product. Insufficient Training Users may not feel confident using the platform effectively. Process Misalignment The product may require workflow adjustments that the organization has not yet implemented. Expectation Gaps Customers may expect results faster than realistically possible.  CSM Actions to Improve Time to Value Define Success Early Align with the customer on clear goals and measurable outcomes.
