Create an Onboarding Plan Develop structured implementation milestones and timelines. Focus on Early Wins Help customers achieve quick, meaningful results that demonstrate value. Provide Enablement and Training Ensure users understand how to integrate the product into their workflows. Track Progress and Communicate Milestones Regularly highlight achievements and improvements resulting from the platform.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate time to value include: • time to first meaningful product use • time to first completed workflow • onboarding milestone completion • early adoption metrics • time to measurable business outcome Tracking these metrics helps organizations understand how quickly customers begin benefiting from the product.

Example Scenario A company implements a cloud security platform to improve visibility into network threats. During onboarding, the Customer Success team focuses on enabling a core monitoring workflow within the first few weeks. Once the customer begins detecting and responding to threats using the platform, they quickly recognize the platform’s value and increase their engagement with additional features.  Strategic Insight Customers rarely abandon a product that has already delivered clear value. The faster a customer experiences meaningful outcomes, the more likely they are to remain committed to the platform and invest further in its capabilities. Customer Success teams that focus on accelerating time to value create stronger relationships and reduce the likelihood of early churn.

Success Planning Definition Success planning is the process of collaboratively defining, documenting, and tracking the goals a customer aims to achieve using a product or service. In Customer Success, a success plan outlines the customer’s desired business outcomes, the steps required to achieve them, and the responsibilities shared between the customer and the vendor. A success plan serves as a strategic roadmap that guides adoption, aligns stakeholders, and ensures progress toward measurable outcomes.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Success planning helps transform the customer relationship from a transactional vendor interaction into a strategic partnership focused on achieving measurable results. Effective success planning allows Customer Success teams to: • align product capabilities with customer business goals • create a structured approach to product adoption • maintain executive alignment • track progress toward measurable outcomes • identify risks or opportunities early When customers clearly understand how the product supports their goals, they are more likely to remain engaged and committed to the partnership.  Customer Psychology Behind Success Planning Customers are more likely to remain invested in a product when they feel there is a clear plan guiding their progress and success. Several psychological factors influence the effectiveness of success planning: Goal clarity Clear goals help stakeholders understand what success looks like and how the product contributes to their objectives.

Accountability Defined responsibilities encourage both the customer and vendor to remain committed to achieving outcomes. Progress visibility Tracking milestones helps customers see improvements and maintain confidence in the product. Strategic alignment When goals align with organizational priorities, the product becomes more relevant to leadership and decision makers. A well-structured success plan helps reinforce the perception that the vendor is actively invested in the customer’s success.  Key Components of a Success Plan Customer Objectives The business goals the customer hopes to achieve using the product. Success Metrics Quantifiable indicators that measure progress toward those goals. Implementation Milestones Specific steps required to deploy and adopt the product effectively. Roles and Responsibilities Identification of key stakeholders responsible for various aspects of the plan. Timeline and Review Cadence Defined checkpoints where progress is evaluated and adjustments are made. These components create a shared understanding of expectations and progress.  Signals of an Effective Success Plan • clear alignment between product capabilities and customer goals • active participation from stakeholders across the organization • measurable progress toward defined outcomes • regular review of milestones and achievements • strong engagement during success meetings or business reviews

These signals indicate that the customer and vendor are working collaboratively toward shared objectives.  Signals of Weak Success Planning • unclear or undefined customer goals • lack of measurable success metrics • limited stakeholder involvement • inconsistent progress tracking • success meetings focused only on product features rather than outcomes Weak success planning can lead to confusion about the product’s value and reduce engagement.  CSM Actions to Strengthen Success Planning Define Goals Early Work with the customer to identify their most important business objectives. Align Product Capabilities with Outcomes Connect specific platform features to the customer’s goals. Track Milestones and Progress Regularly review achievements and adjust the plan as necessary. Engage Stakeholders Ensure that both operational users and executive leaders understand the success plan. Revisit the Plan Periodically Update the plan as the customer’s priorities evolve or new opportunities emerge.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help measure the effectiveness of success planning include: • progress toward defined customer outcomes • adoption of key product features • milestone completion rates • engagement with success meetings or reviews • improvement in operational or business performance metrics

Monitoring these indicators helps ensure that the success plan remains aligned with the customer’s goals.  Example Scenario A company adopts a cloud monitoring platform to improve infrastructure visibility and reduce system downtime. During onboarding, the Customer Success team works with the customer to define success goals such as reducing incident response time and improving system reliability. The success plan outlines implementation milestones, training sessions, and performance metrics. Over time, the customer achieves measurable improvements and continues expanding their use of the platform.  Strategic Insight A product alone does not guarantee customer success. Customers succeed when there is a clear strategy connecting the product to their desired outcomes. Success planning ensures that both the customer and the vendor remain focused on achieving meaningful results rather than simply using the platform.

Time to Value Definition Time to Value (TTV) refers to the amount of time it takes for a customer to realize meaningful benefits from a product or service after implementation. In Customer Success, reducing time to value is critical because early positive outcomes reinforce the customer’s confidence in the product and vendor relationship. Time to Value is closely connected to onboarding, product adoption, and the customer’s ability to integrate the platform into their workflows.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Customers often purchase software with the expectation that it will quickly improve their operations, solve problems, or deliver measurable outcomes. If customers do not experience value within a reasonable timeframe, they may begin questioning the effectiveness of the solution. Reducing time to value helps Customer Success teams: • accelerate product adoption • increase customer satisfaction • strengthen early customer confidence • reduce churn risk • create opportunities for expansion Early success builds momentum and encourages customers to deepen their investment in the platform.  Customer Psychology Behind Time to Value Customers typically evaluate new technologies based on how quickly they see tangible results. When value is delayed, uncertainty and skepticism may increase. Several psychological factors influence perceptions of value: Expectation fulfillment Customers expect the product to deliver the benefits promised during the sales process.

Confidence building Early success increases trust in both the product and the vendor. Motivation and momentum Quick wins create positive momentum that encourages users to continue adopting the platform. Perceived investment return Stakeholders want to justify the time, effort, and cost invested in implementing the product. When customers see early results, they become more engaged and motivated to expand their usage.  Signals of Strong Time to Value • customers achieving early milestones during onboarding • successful completion of initial workflows • measurable improvements in operational efficiency • positive feedback shortly after implementation • increasing product adoption during early stages These signals suggest that customers are beginning to experience meaningful benefits from the platform.  Signals of Delayed Time to Value • long or stalled implementation timelines • incomplete onboarding processes • users unsure how to apply the product to their workflows • declining engagement shortly after onboarding • customers questioning the product’s value early in the relationship Delayed value realization may increase the likelihood of churn.  Common Root Causes Complex Implementation Integration challenges or technical dependencies may slow deployment.

Unclear Success Criteria Customers may not clearly understand what success looks like with the product. Insufficient Training Users may not feel confident using the platform effectively. Process Misalignment The product may require workflow adjustments that the organization has not yet implemented. Expectation Gaps Customers may expect results faster than realistically possible.  CSM Actions to Improve Time to Value Define Success Early Align with the customer on clear goals and measurable outcomes. Create an Onboarding Plan Develop structured implementation milestones and timelines. Focus on Early Wins Help customers achieve quick, meaningful results that demonstrate value. Provide Enablement and Training Ensure users understand how to integrate the product into their workflows. Track Progress and Communicate Milestones Regularly highlight achievements and improvements resulting from the platform.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate time to value include: • time to first meaningful product use • time to first completed workflow • onboarding milestone completion • early adoption metrics • time to measurable business outcome Tracking these metrics helps organizations understand how quickly customers begin benefiting from the product.

Example Scenario A company implements a cloud security platform to improve visibility into network threats. During onboarding, the Customer Success team focuses on enabling a core monitoring workflow within the first few weeks. Once the customer begins detecting and responding to threats using the platform, they quickly recognize the platform’s value and increase their engagement with additional features.  Strategic Insight Customers rarely abandon a product that has already delivered clear value. The faster a customer experiences meaningful outcomes, the more likely they are to remain committed to the platform and invest further in its capabilities. Customer Success teams that focus on accelerating time to value create stronger relationships

Customer Journey Definition The customer journey refers to the complete lifecycle of a customer’s experience with a product or service, from the initial purchase and onboarding process through adoption, value realization, renewal, and potential expansion. In Customer Success, understanding the customer journey helps teams guide customers through each stage of their relationship with the vendor. Each stage of the journey presents different opportunities, risks, and engagement strategies that influence long-term customer success.  Why It Matters in Customer Success A structured customer journey enables Customer Success teams to proactively manage the customer relationship rather than reacting to issues as they arise. Understanding the customer journey helps organizations: • guide customers through successful onboarding and adoption • anticipate challenges at different stages of the lifecycle • identify opportunities to deliver value and strengthen relationships • reduce churn risk during critical moments • create opportunities for renewal and expansion When Customer Success teams manage the journey effectively, customers are more likely to achieve meaningful outcomes and remain long-term partners.  Customer Psychology Throughout the Journey Customer expectations and priorities often change throughout the lifecycle of the relationship. Recognizing these shifts allows Customer Success teams to tailor their approach at each stage. Early Stage – Excitement and Expectations Customers are optimistic about the product and expect quick improvements or results. Adoption Stage – Learning and Adjustment Users begin integrating the product into their workflows and may encounter challenges or resistance.

Value Realization Stage – Confidence Building Customers begin recognizing measurable outcomes and feel more confident in their investment. Renewal Stage – Evaluation and Justification Customers evaluate whether the product continues to deliver value relative to its cost. Understanding these psychological transitions helps Customer Success teams provide the right support at the right time.  Key Stages of the Customer Journey Onboarding The initial implementation and setup of the product. This stage focuses on helping customers understand how the platform works and preparing them to begin using it effectively. Adoption Customers integrate the product into their daily workflows and begin using key features regularly. Value Realization Customers start seeing measurable improvements or outcomes that demonstrate the product’s impact. Renewal Customers evaluate whether to continue their investment in the platform based on the value they have experienced. Expansion Customers deepen their investment by increasing usage, adding new features, or expanding adoption across teams.  Signals of a Healthy Customer Journey • smooth onboarding and implementation • increasing product adoption over time • strong engagement with Customer Success teams

• measurable business outcomes achieved with the platform • positive feedback and executive support • interest in additional capabilities or expansion opportunities These signals suggest that the customer relationship is progressing successfully through the lifecycle.  Signals of Journey Breakdown • stalled onboarding or implementation delays • limited adoption of key product features • declining engagement with Customer Success teams • lack of measurable value or outcomes • customers questioning the product’s relevance during renewal discussions Breakdowns at earlier stages often create risk later in the lifecycle.  CSM Actions to Guide the Customer Journey Establish Clear Onboarding Milestones Define implementation goals and timelines to help customers reach initial success quickly. Support Product Adoption Provide training and enablement resources to help users integrate the product into their workflows. Track and Communicate Value Demonstrate measurable outcomes that reinforce the product’s impact. Maintain Regular Engagement Schedule success meetings, strategy sessions, and QBRs to maintain alignment. Prepare for Renewal Early Ensure customers recognize the product’s value well before renewal discussions begin.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators that help evaluate progress through the customer journey include:

• onboarding completion rates • time to value • product adoption metrics • engagement levels • customer health scores • renewal and expansion indicators Tracking these metrics helps Customer Success teams identify where customers are in the lifecycle and where additional support may be required.  Example Scenario A company adopts a new analytics platform to improve operational visibility. During onboarding, the Customer Success team helps implement core dashboards and train key users. Over time, adoption spreads across departments as teams recognize the platform’s ability to streamline reporting and decision-making. As measurable improvements become clear, executives support renewal and expand the platform to additional business units.  Strategic Insight Customer success does not happen at a single moment—it develops across the entire lifecycle of the relationship. Organizations that intentionally guide customers through each stage of the journey are far more likely to achieve strong retention, deeper adoption, and long-term partnerships.

Voice of the Customer (VoC) Definition Voice of the Customer (VoC) refers to the process of collecting, analyzing, and acting on customer feedback to better understand their experiences, expectations, and needs. In Customer Success, VoC programs help organizations capture insights directly from customers and use those insights to improve products, services, and the overall customer experience. VoC ensures that customer perspectives are continuously incorporated into business decisions and product development.  Why It Matters in Customer Success Customer Success teams are often the closest point of contact between the vendor and the customer. This position allows CSMs to capture valuable insights about how customers use the product, what challenges they face, and what improvements they would like to see. Effective VoC programs help organizations: • understand customer needs and expectations • identify product improvement opportunities • detect potential churn risks early • improve customer satisfaction • strengthen customer relationships When organizations actively listen to customers and respond to feedback, they demonstrate commitment to continuous improvement.  Customer Psychology Behind VoC Customers are more likely to share feedback when they believe their opinions will be heard and acted upon. Several psychological dynamics influence customer feedback: Recognition and validation Customers want to feel that their experiences and perspectives are valued.

Desire for improvement Customers often provide feedback to help improve the tools they rely on. Frustration expression Negative feedback may reflect unresolved issues or unmet expectations. Partnership mentality Customers who feel like partners in the product’s development are more engaged in providing feedback. Customer Success teams play a key role in creating an environment where customers feel comfortable sharing honest input.  Common Sources of Voice of the Customer Data Customer Surveys Structured surveys such as NPS or satisfaction surveys provide measurable feedback. Customer Interviews Direct conversations with customers reveal deeper insights into their experiences and challenges. Support Tickets Recurring support issues often highlight product limitations or usability challenges. Business Reviews Strategic discussions with customers frequently uncover feedback about product performance and value. User Communities and Forums Customer communities often reveal trends in feedback and feature requests.  Signals of Strong VoC Engagement • customers actively providing feedback or suggestions • participation in surveys or interviews • willingness to join advisory boards or user groups • constructive feedback during business reviews • ongoing dialogue about product improvements These signals indicate that customers feel engaged and believe their input matters.

Signals of Weak VoC Engagement • low participation in surveys or feedback initiatives • limited communication about product experiences • customers expressing frustration without providing details • lack of structured processes for capturing customer insights Weak VoC engagement may indicate that customers feel their feedback is not valued or acted upon.  CSM Actions to Strengthen VoC Programs Encourage Open Feedback Create opportunities for customers to share honest perspectives about the product. Capture and Document Insights Record recurring themes, feature requests, and challenges. Share Feedback Internally Communicate customer insights to product, support, and leadership teams. Close the Feedback Loop Inform customers when their feedback influences product improvements. Identify Strategic Trends Look for patterns across accounts that indicate broader customer needs.  Metrics to Monitor Indicators used to evaluate VoC effectiveness include: • survey response rates • Net Promoter Score (NPS) trends • number of feature requests or feedback submissions • participation in advisory programs • improvements in customer satisfaction over time These metrics help organizations measure how effectively they capture and act on customer insights.

Example Scenario A software company receives repeated feedback from customers that reporting features are difficult to customize. Customer Success teams document this feedback across multiple accounts and share the insights with the product team. After improvements are implemented, customers report higher satisfaction and improved usability.  Strategic Insight Customer feedback is one of the most valuable sources of insight for improving products and services. Organizations that actively listen to customers and incorporate their feedback into decision-making are better positioned to build stronger relationships, improve products, and maintain long-term customer loyalty.
